summary: This guest post by Eugene Stern argues that virtual reality poses an amplified threat because it scales up the most troublesome aspects of existing technology: personalization, addictiveness, and marketing manipulation. VR will collect unprecedented amounts of biometric and behavioral data through eye tracking, heart rate monitoring, and brain activity sensors, enabling hyper-targeted manipulation. The immersive nature of VR will make virtual experiences far more addictive than smartphones or social media. Most concerning, VR advertising will achieve unprecedented precision in measuring and predicting consumer behavior. The post concludes that VR's greatest danger is that it represents 'more of the same, but on steroids' - amplifying problems we've already failed to address.
key_points: VR massively scales up both data collection capabilities (eye tracking, biometrics, brain activity) and the complexity of personalized manipulation possible with that data
The addictiveness of current technology will seem quaint compared to personalized VR sex and immersive social simulations
VR advertising will achieve unprecedented effectiveness by precisely measuring physiological responses and predicting which ads will work for each individual
VR could be a 'great leveler' offering virtual access to elite experiences, making constant marketing seem like a small price to pay
Society has shown no ability to confront technology addiction, surveillance, or manipulation - VR will make all of these ten times more powerful
money_quotes: In a contest between your family, friends, and VR set over who knows you better, it's hard to see the humans having a chance.
VR is going to make the virtual world way more addictive.
The internet is funded by advertising. Why wouldn't VR be?
VR might enable advertisers to fully exploit you economically, to optimize and control all of your purchasing power.
We haven't been troubled yet whenever our technology asks us to give up control to gain comfort.
The scariest thing about VR might be that it could be more of the same, but on steroids.

If we've shown no societal ability so far to confront technology addiction, data collection and surveillance, or media manipulation, what happens when VR technology renders all of these ten times more powerful?
key_insight: VR's greatest danger isn't that it introduces new problems, but that it dramatically amplifies existing technology harms - surveillance, addiction, and manipulation - that society has already proven unable to confront.
